headline: The most collectable Christmas cards – from $2 to $14,000
summary: Season’s greetings can become a sound investment
body_text:
In 1843, Christmas spirit was at an all-time high. Prince Albert’s penchant for Christmas trees was beginning to set a precedent in Victorian Britain and Charles Dickens’ A Christmas Carol would be published that December; the first edition sold out within a week. That same season, Henry Cole, civil servant and founding director of the V&A, commissioned the first commercially sold Christmas card, illustrated and hand-coloured with a festive family celebration. Priced at one shilling (about £3 in today’s money) and featuring a controversial depiction of underage drinking, the card was a flop. Almost 180 years later, however, one of the remaining 21 copies was snapped up at Christie’s for £13,750, outstripping a high estimate of £8,000.

Today the design belongs to the rich collectors’ market that exploded after Cole’s invention. “Christmas cards took off in a huge way in the late 1870s,” explains Joanna Espin, senior curator at London’s Postal Museum. By 1879, the Post Office was report
